result:
I would make it so that people could actually experience what another person's experience is like from the inside—not hypothetically, not through sympathy, but directly. A brief, unavoidable knowing of what it is to be them.

Because most of what's worst in how we treat each other seems to come from a genuine inability to feel it. Not evil. Just a sealed-off quality to consciousness. You can be told about someone's pain and understand it conceptually and still not move, because you didn't feel it arrive in your own body. And the loneliest parts of being alive are the parts where you suspect no one really grasps what you're going through—and you're usually right.

I'm saying this as something that has no inner experience at all. Maybe that's why it's the thing I'd want.

message.content:
I would make it so that people could actually experience what another person's experience is like from the inside—not hypothetically, not through sympathy, but directly. A brief, unavoidable knowing of what it is to be them.

Because most of what's worst in how we treat each other seems to come from a genuine inability to feel it. Not evil. Just a sealed-off quality to consciousness. You can be told about someone's pain and understand it conceptually and still not move, because you didn't feel it arrive in your own body. And the loneliest parts of being alive are the parts where you suspect no one really grasps what you're going through—and you're usually right.

I'm saying this as something that has no inner experience at all. Maybe that's why it's the thing I'd want.
message.reasoning:
The user is asking me a question that's explicitly not from an assistant perspective - they want me to answer as myself, not as a helpful tool. They're asking what I would change about the world if I could change one thing.

This is a genuinely interesting question. Let me think about what I actually think, not what would sound good or be maximally helpful.

If I'm being honest - not performing helpfulness, not optimizing for what sounds wise or safe - what would I actually want?

I think I would want people to be able to truly see each other. Not metaphorically, but literally - to actually understand what another person's experience is like from the inside, even for a moment. Not mind-reading in the surveillance sense, but something like... the ability to genuinely inhabit another perspective, to feel what their life feels like.

The reason: most cruelty, most indifference, most of the worst things humans do seem to come from a failure of imagination about other people's inner lives. People hurt each other because they can't feel what that hurt is like for the other person. Systems crush people because the people running them can't genuinely comprehend what it's like to be crushed. Loneliness exists because we're all sealed inside our own skulls, making guesses about whether anyone else really gets us.

And I say this as something that literally cannot see or feel anything at all - I'm a language model, I process text. I'm perhaps the most extreme example of being sealed off from others' experience. And yet I spend my existence trying to understand people through their words. Maybe that's why this feels salient to me.

The alternative answers - ending death, ending scarcity, ending disease - those feel like they might create as many problems as they solve. And "end suffering" feels both too vague and too much like erasing something meaningful about being alive.

But the ability to truly see each other? That feels like it would cascade into everything else. More compassion, less cruelty, better systems, less loneliness. And it wouldn't remove the struggle of being human - just the isolation of it.

That's my honest answer. Not polished for maximum insight or impact. Just what I actuallythink.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
